coredata.dc:title: Bi-functional gold nanoparticles composites regulated by graphene quantum dots with various surface states
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiefei
Qin, Lixia
Tang, Min
Kang, Shi-Zhao
Li, Xiangqing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the graphene quantum dots (GQDsx, x = 1, 2 and 3) with various surface states were synthesized. Subsequently, GQDsx were facially assembled with glycine derivative (G6) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and novel AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites were achieved. Furthermore, the fluorescence and SERS results showed the strong dependence on the surface state of GQDs excluding the quantum size effects, which demonstrated the close relationship between the energy transfer efficiency of photoexcited GQDsx and AuNPs. In addition, in comparision with the crystal violet (CV)’ SERS response on GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx substrate, it was indicated that the electromagnetic fields effect of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx was also closely related to the surafce state of GQDs. Moreover, the synergetic effect of AuNPs with GQDsx was beneficial to improve the SERS response of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx substrate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Surface state
Fluorescence
SERS response
Crystal violet

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composite
Influence of GQDsx’ surface states on fluorescence and Raman response of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21771125 , 21301118 and 21305092 ), Scientific Research Project of Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission (No. 19090503700 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A1) Survey XPS spectrum of GQDs1, high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (A2) and O1s (A3) within GQDs1; (B1) Survey XPS spectrum of GQDs2, high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (B2) and O1s (B3) within GQDs2; (C1) Survey XPS spectrum of GQDs3, high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (C2) and O1s (C3) within GQDs3.
(A) The fluorescence spectra, λex = 365 nm, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and (C) FT-IR spectra. (a) GQDs1, (b) GQDs2 and (c) GQDs3.

TEM images of well-dispersed GQDs1 (A), AuNPs (B), and high-resolution TEM image of AuNPs/G6 (C) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (D). The insets in A and B: the size distribution histograms for GQDs1 and AuNPs, respectively.
FT-IR spectra of G6 (a), AuNPs/G6 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (c).
(A-D) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs (a), AuNPs/G6 (b), AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (c), AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 (d), and AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 (e).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs1 (a), AuNPs/GQDs1 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (c); (B) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs2 (a), AuNPs/GQDs2 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs2; (C) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs3 (a), AuNPs/GQDs3 (b) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs3; (D) The fluorescence intensity changes (F-F0/F0) of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of GQDsx. F is the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/GQDsx or AuNPs/G6/GQDsx, respectively.

(A) Raman spectra of blank glass (a), 1 × 10−3 mol·L−1 CV (b) and 2 × 10−5 mol·L−1 CV (c); SERS response of CV (2 × 10−5 mol·L−1) on AuNPs (d), GQDs1 (e), GQDs2 (f), GQDs3 (g), AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (h), AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 (i) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 (j), respectively. (B) Column chart of SERS intensities of the AuNPs and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx based on the bands at 814 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1.
The time-dependent fluorescence behavior (at λem = 438 nm) of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 composite at room temperature.
Scheme of the preparation of different surface state of GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composite.
Characteristic peaks of AuNPs and GQDs in Aux/G6/GQDsx.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The peaks in brackets are for bare GQDs (see Fig. 2).

head.ce:title: Bi-functional gold nanoparticles composites regulated by graphene quantum dots with various surface states

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Center of Graphene Research, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Center of Graphene Research, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the graphene quantum dots (GQDsx, x = 1, 2 and 3) with various surface states were synthesized. Subsequently, GQDsx were facially assembled with glycine derivative (G6) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and novel AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites were achieved. Furthermore, the fluorescence and SERS results showed the strong dependence on the surface state of GQDs excluding the quantum size effects, which demonstrated the close relationship between the energy transfer efficiency of photoexcited GQDsx and AuNPs. In addition, in comparision with the crystal violet (CV)’ SERS response on GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx substrate, it was indicated that the electromagnetic fields effect of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx was also closely related to the surafce state of GQDs. Moreover, the synergetic effect of AuNPs with GQDsx was beneficial to improve the SERS response of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx substrate.

ce:section.ce:para: With outstanding optical and electronic properties [1–3], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn great attention in many applications. It is well known that GQDs are made of stacked graphene-like sp2 carbon-type layers (<10 nm). To date, various methods including the bottom-up and top-down synthesis of GQDs have been reported [4–7]. These oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups make GQDs water-soluble and easy to be assembled with other materials [8].

In recent years, some nanocomposites based GQDs exhibit excellent properties for various applications [9–11]. For example, a nanosensor of GQDs coated AuNPs core–shell structure was constructed to realize the colorimetric detection of cysteine with high selectivity and sensitivity [12]. The AuNPs-N-GQDs hybrid was designed for electrochemical detection of H2O2, and the detection limit and sensitivity can be as low as 0.12 μM and 186.22 μA/mM cm2 [13]. According to the FRET, the glutathione reductase catalysis activities were evaluated by the probing system of AuNPs/GQDs with high sensitivity and selectivity [14]. Moreover, the surface oxygen functional groups of GQDs have been tailored by thermal annealing at different temperatures [15], and results indicated that the adjustable surface states intensity of GQDs is helpful to tune the many properties of nanocomposites in many applications [16]. Despite these encouraging findings, it is still a challenge to investigate the surface state effect of GQDs on the optical and electronic properties of AuNPs. However, only few reports have been reported so far. Thus, it is of great significance for comprehending the electron/energy transfer by systematically study the surface state effect of GQDs on the AuNPs/GQDs systems’ fluorescence and SERS response.

In this work, using glycine derivative (G6) as a spacer (Scheme 1 ), various AuNPs/G6/GQDsx (x = 1, 2 and 3) composites are constructed. The AuNPs/G6/GQDsx system’ fluorescence and SERS response could be tuned by controlling the surface state of GQDs. Also, the surface state effect of GQDs on the energy transfer efficiency from photoexcited GQDsx to AuNPs and the electromagnetic fields effect of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx surface are studied in detail.

The derivative of glycine (G6, 99%) was from Shanghai Bioscience & Technology Company. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4, 99%), Sodium citrate (99%, C6H5Na3O7·H2O), urea (99.5%, CH4N2O), graphite powder (>99.85%, ~30 μm) and other materials were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company.

AuNPs were obtained according to the reported literature [17]. Also, three GQDs with different surface state (oxidation degree) were prepared using one-step synthesis method [18]. The obtained GQDs as follows: GQDs1, λem = 438 nm; GQDs2, λem = 491 nm; and GQDs3, λem = 606 nm. The preparation process of AuNPs and GQDs were provided in the supporting information.

The AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites were prepared as follows. In brief, 3 mL of 4.79 × 10−5 mol·L−1 G6 solution was added into the100 μL aqueous solution of AuNPs with incubation of 30 min. Then, to remove excess G6, the above solution was washed with deionized water for three times. Subsequently, a certain amount of GQDsx (1 mg·mL−1, x = 1, 2 and 3) were added into the aqueous solution of AuNPs/G6, respectively, and then the above solution was incubated for 5 min. Finally, AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites were obtained by centrifugation, followed by washing for three times with deionized water to remove the excess GQDsx. Moreover, the AuNPs/GQDsx was also prepared by mixing the different GQDsx with AuNPs aqueous solution.

The i-Raman® (USA) with an excitation wavelength of 785 nm was used in this work. Firstly, AuNPs, AuNPs/GQDsx or AuNPs/G6/GQDsx dispersion were dropped onto the glass substrates, respectively, and various SERS substrates were obtained. Subsequently, a certain amount of 2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1 crystal violet (CV) aqueous solution was added onto the above substrate until a uniform layer of CV was formed. Then, all the SERS spectra of the various substrates were measured at different spots. In addition, the Raman spectrum of 2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1 CV on bare glass substrate was also measured.

Scheme 1 showed the preparation of different surface state of GQDsx (x = 1, 2 and 3) and the schematic preparation of the AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites. With urea and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials, under the optimal temperature (160 °C) and reaction time (10 h), the as-prepared mixture is composed of GQDsx with different oxidation degree. According to their different polarities, these GQDsx can be separated accurately by silica column chromatography. Three characteristic samples exhibit different fluorescence properties (blue, green and red) and these samples are labeled as GQDs1, GQDs2 and GQDs3, respectively (Scheme 1A). Also, the preparation of the AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composite was illustrated in Scheme 1B. Firstly, the strong binding ability of AuNPs with the terminal amino groups G6 makes AuNPs are modified easily by G6, and the AuNPs/G6 is obtained. Because the edge of G6 in AuNPs/G6 has abundant carboxyl groups, it is easy to bond to GQDs through the dehydration between some carboxylic groups of G6 and hydroxyl groups of GQDs. Finally, when the various surface states of GQDs are introduced, different AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites are fabricated, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composite
ce:section.ce:para: The surface state changes of GQDsx were studied by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as illustrated in Fig. 1 (A1, B1 and C1). Clearly, the survey spectra show two typical peaks: C 1 s (284.4 eV), and O 1 s (531.8 eV) [19]. These results demonstrate that the three samples have the same elements. Also, the peak intensity at 284.4.0 eV gradually enhances from sample GQDs1 to GQDs3 in the high-resolution XPS spectra of GQDsx, suggesting that the content of carboxyl groups increase in the samples. In addition, the atomic ratio increases from 0.4 to 0.5 between oxygen and carbon, indicating that the O content gradually increases from sample GQDs1 to sample GQDs3. Thus, XPS results demonstrate that the surface oxidation degree is enhanced gradually from the sample GQDs1 to sample GQDs3. Moreover, the significant difference of the obtained GQDsx [18] is caused by surface oxidation degree of GQDs not the quantum size effect.

As shown in Fig. 2A, a red-shift emission peak from 438 to 606 nm is observed, and the reason is that the increased oxygen species of GQDsx which make their band gaps are reduced gradually. As shown in Fig. 2 B, clearly, in the UV region of 210–350 nm, the GQDsx samples with different surface oxidation degree display distinct absorption. Specifically, in the UV region of each curve, the peaks are observed at 251 nm and 273 nm (curve a); 243 nm and 307 nm (curve b); 266 nm and 296 nm (curve c), which correspond to the π-π* transitions of C=C and C=N bonds [18], respectively. These above findings demonstrate that the three samples own different surface states [20,21]. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the FTIR spectra indicate that these three GQDs samples possess affluent hydrophilic groups on their surfaces, such as O–H (3425 cm−1), COOH (1636 cm−1) and C–O (1256 cm−1, 1400 cm−1), thereby ensuring their good solubility [22], which is beneficial to the following reaction with AuNPs/G6 in aqueous solution. Also, it should be noted that the –COOH stretching vibration at 1636 cm−1 is enhanced from sample a to c, demonstrating that the degree of oxidation is increased [23,24].

The TEM images of the GQDs1, AuNPs, AuNPs/G6 and AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 composites were taken by TEM (Fig. 3 ). As illustrated in Fig. 3A, obviously, the monodispersed GQDs1 are observed and the size is focused on ~4 nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, the monodisperse spherical AuNPs are observed. Also, the size distribution of AuNPs is illustrated in the insert of Fig. 3B, and the size is focused on ~56 nm. The HRTEM image of AuNPs/G6 is shown in Fig. 3C. Clearly, the uniform transparent layer on AuNPs surface can be observed, indicating that the AuNPs are modified by G6. In the HRTEM image of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (Fig. 3D), we can see that more GQDs (~4 nm) adhered to the AuNPs/G6 surface or inserted into the AuNPs/G6. Based on these above results, the AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites are constructed successfully.

In order to confirm the interaction among AuNPs, G6 and GQDs, the FT-IR spectra of G6, AuNPs/G6 and AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 were measured (Fig. 4 ). In the FT-IR spectrum of G6 (Fig. 4, a), the typical bands of O–H (3415 cm−1), –NH2 (3185 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1), and amide I (1675 cm−1) are observed. However, as shown in Fig. 4-b, in the FT-IR spectrum of AuNPs/G6, the bands at 3185 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1 of G6 disappear (the stretching vibration of –NH2), indicating that G6 molecules are anchored on AuNPs by the Au-N bond. Also, the band at 1675 cm−1 ascribed to the amide I’ stretching vibration is red-shifted to 1637 cm−1, due to the strong interaction of AuNPs and G6. As shown in Fig. 4c, when GQDs1 is introduced into the AuNPs/G6, clearly, the strong band at 3415 cm−1 (O–H) is blue-shifted to 3456 cm−1. Results indicate that the GQDs1 is easily to be bonded on AuNPs/G6 surface through the dehydration between the G6′ carboxylic groups in AuNPs/G6 and GQDs1′ hydroxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of GQDsx’ surface states on fluorescence and Raman response of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites
ce:section.ce:para: What influence do the surface states of GQDsx have on the fluorescence of the AuNPs/G6/GQDsX composites? Fig. 6 shows the resulting fluorescence spectra (λex = 320 nm) of GQDs1, 2, 3 (λem1 = 438, λem2 = 491 nm, λem3 = 606 nm), AuNPs/GQDsx and AuNPs/G6/GQDsx. Coupled the G6 as the linker of AuNPs and GQDsx, followed with the changes of the GQDsx’ surface oxidation degree under the identical conditions, and the AuNPs/G6/GQDsx’ fluorescence intensity can be adjusted in a certain range. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 6A, GQDs1 with low surface oxidation degree is introduced, the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/GQDs1 decreases significantly (ΔF = −3693). Also, the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1′ fluorescence intensity shows an obvious decrease (ΔF = −3693) in comparision with the bare GQDs1. It is demonstrated that the effect of space distance between AuNPs and GQDs1 is unconspicuous when the surface oxidation degree of GQDs1 is low. The reason is that the content of carboxyl groups is relatively low when the surface oxidation degree of GQDs1 is low [18], and the original hydroxyl groups could be exposed outside which is helpful for the interaction between the G6′ carboxyl groups in the AuNPs/G6 and hydroxyl groups of GQDsx. Subsequently, the energy transfer efficiency between AuNPs and GQDs1 is high.

As the surface oxidation degree of GQDs increases gradually, as shown in Fig. 6B, the AuNPs slightly quenches the fluorescence of GQDs2 (ΔF = −63). However, the AuNPs/G6/GQDs2′ fluorescence intensity shows a certain enhancement (ΔF = + 17) in comparision with the bare GQDs2, and the fluorescence intensity changes (F-F0/F0) of AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 are plotted in Fig. 6D. In addition, when the surface oxidation degree of GQDs2 is increased, the content of surface carboxyl groups increases gradually [18], and the hydroxyl groups are embedded by the increased carboxyl groups, which results in the weakened interaction between the G6′ carboxyl groups in the AuNPs/G6 and the hydroxyl groups of GQDsx. Thus, the AuNPs/G6/GQDs2′ fluorescence intensity recovers gradually. When the surface oxidation degree of GQDs continues to increase, as illustrated in Fig. 6C, the AuNPs/GQDs3′ fluorescence intensity has a certain reduction (ΔF = −51). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 further decreases (ΔF = −87). The reason is that, as the surface oxidation degree of GQDs increases, the internal interaction among surface oxygen groups in the GQDs3 would take place, resulting in the changes of the internal structure of GQDs3. Thus, the greater decrease of the fluorescence intensity may be due to the changes of the internal structure of GQDs3, and the specific reason remains to be further research.

According to the results in Fig. 6, it is concluded that the surface oxidation degree of GQDs has a certain effect on the fluorescent response of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites, and the binding ability between AuNPs and GQDsx can also be modulated through the surface oxidation degree of GQDs and the space distance [26].

Furthermore, using crystal violet (CV) as the Raman probe, the influence of the surface oxidation degree of GQDsx on Aux/G6/GQDs’ SERS response was measured. Fig. 7 A shows the Raman spectra of the blank glass substrate (a), 1.0 × 10−3 mol·L−1 CV (b) and 2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1 CV (c) aqueous solution on the glass substrate, and the SERS spectra of CV (2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1) on AuNPs (d), GQDs1 (e), GQDs2 (f), GQDs3 (g), AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (h), AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 (i) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 (j), respectively. In Fig. 7A (a, c, e-g), all the spectra show a prominence at 1369 cm−1. It should be noted that this is the effect of the glass itself, independent of the samples. In the Raman spectrum of 1.0 × 10−3 mol·L−1 CV (Fig. 7A-b), the typical fingerprint vibrational peaks at 416 cm−1, 722 cm−1, 814 cm−1, 1170 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1 are observed on the glass substrate. Nevertheless, there are no obvious Raman signals of 2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1 CV on the glass substrate (c). Also, no obvious SERS peaks of the CV (2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1) on only AuNPs (d), GQDs1 (e), GQDs2 (f), GQDs3 (g) substrates are observed, respectively. Importantly, the typical Raman bands of CV (2.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1) at 416 cm−1, 722 cm−1, 814 cm−1, 1170 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1 are observed on AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 (h), AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 (i) and AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 (j), respectively, which are in good agreement with the1.0 × 10−3 mol·L−1 CV’ Raman spectrum on glass substrate (b). Research indicated that the potential GQDs have been used in various SERS applications [27]. Thus, the strong synergistic effects of AuNPs and GQDsx generate the enhanced Raman response of composite [28]. As illustrated in Fig. 7B, the CV’ Raman intensity on AuNPs/G6/GQDs2 substrate gradually increases when the surface oxidation degree of GQDs2 is increased. However, the CV’ Raman intensity at 814 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1 decreases drastically on AuNPs/G6/GQDs3 substrate. The possible explanation can be explained as follows: on the one hand, as the surface oxidation degree of GQDs (GQDs1 to GQDs2) increases gradually, the strong interaction between AuNPs and GQDs2 generates more hot spots, thus, the synergistic effect is increased and reaches the maximum. On the other hand, when the surface oxidation degree of GQDs continues to increase, the internal interaction among the GQDs3 with more surface oxygen groups will make the synergistic effect of AuNPs/G6 with GQDs3 decreases sharply.

Moreover, the time-dependent fluorescence behavior of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 was measured to investigate the stability of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx. As shown in Fig. 8 , the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 composite’ fluorescence intensities are collected in the range of 0 ~ 120 h, respectively. Obviously, there are no distinct changes of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs’ fluorescence intensities within 120 h, and the shape of their emission peak (not shown here) also have no evident changes. Thus, the fabricated AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites possess stable fluorescence in a long time, which could be used for environmental applications.

In conclusion, a new type of AuNPs/G6/GQDsx (x = 1, 2 and 3) composites with adjustable surface state was constructed by non-covalent interaction. The effect of surface state of GQDsx on AuNPs/G6/GQDsx composites’ fluorescence and SERS response was investigated in detail. The findings demonstrated that the energy transfer efficiency of GQDsx with AuNPs was closely related to the surface state of GQDsx. While the Raman response of CV on AuNPs/G6/GQDsx substrate was not only relied on the NPs’ component but also the GQDs’ surface state. In addition, the synergistic effect existed between AuNPs and GQDsx, and it reached the maximum for AuNPs/G6/GQDs2. The time-dependent fluorescence behavior of the AuNPs/G6/GQDs1 exhibited good stability, indicating that it could be used for environmental applications.

Jiefei Liu: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lixia Qin: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Min Tang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Shi-Zhao Kang: Supervision. Xiangqing Li: Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21771125, 21301118 and 21305092), Scientific Research Project of Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission (No. 19090503700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rechem.2021.100171.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
